regions.label: text
regions.text:
part an Asset to the Race

The common conception of a life insurance company is, that it is something that functions only when a man has passed away. The public has little knowledge of the tremendous influence that legal reserve insurance companies have on the life of the nation, in the preservation and conservation of human life, as a backbone of the financial system, the country and as a protector of business and industry.

Spend 'Thousands Fighting Diseases' Insurance companies spend thousands of dollars each year fighting diseases and nursing back to health the stricken. It is to their financial interest to produce longevity in the human race, which is likewise the chief interest and desire of every, individual. While we often see the insurance nursing staff administering to the elderly the vast amount of money, time and brain that insurance companies use to prevent disease.

regions.label: paragraph_title
regions.text: Control Finance of Nation

regions.label: text
regions.text:
Legal reserve life insurance companies control life finance in the United States than all the national, state and private banks combined. A majority of the first mortgages on farms are financed by insurance company money. Bonds of railroad companies, industries, cities and institutions on farms are documented for the large reserve that is accumulated.

To Meet Local Needs

The Liberty Life Insurance Company, a legal reserve company, will be to the Negro group in this country what the white companies have been to their people. The entire capital of $100,000 will be invested in high-grade mortgage on improved property purchased by Colored people. This great sum will be utilized to improve contributions of the Negro in Chicago and other places where stockholders live. It will no longer be necessary for Colored men to have to give up good options on high-grade property because of objections from white neighbors to control the channels of financial aid the Negro gets larger and larger the funds will increase well protected bonds issued by Colored business firms, thus building up for ourselves industrial and commercial endeavors. The Standard Life Insurance Company of Atlanta, Georgia, which is similar to the Liberty Life, has amassed a half million dollar reorganization of this character, have had an awakening and are purchasing options on stock as they become available in the Liberty Life Insurance Company, whose combined capital and plus is larger than any other Negro investment in the world, will soon begin issuing policies and investing the funds that the large body of stockholders have massed. The business and executive ability of the board of directors is an assurance to the public that every insurance company has a good which legal reserve for the Nation will be exercised by the Liberty Life Insurance Company toward our people.

regions.label: paragraph_title
regions.text: Applications Received

regions.label: text
regions.text:
Applications are still being received for stock in the company upon options which have become available through the industrial depression. A revival of business, which is slowly coming, will wipe out these opportunities in a very short time. Payments on notes held by the company are being paid as they fall due, so that the value of subscriptions will necessarily be discontinued after a short time. The public character of the work of a company of this nature recommends it as an investment to Race-loving people. (Advertisement.)

Will Celebrate Anniversary
The 25th anniversary of the marriage of Rev. Benjamin Brownlee and wife will be celebrated on Jan. 25 at M. Aratrant Baptist church, 3337 South State street.

regions.label: paragraph_title
regions.text: Returns to St. Paul

regions.label: text
regions.text:
Mrs. Mary E. Brown of St. Paul, Minn., who was called here for the funeral of her daughter, Mrs. Addie Crawford Ridley, has returned to her home.

Hudson-Wheeler Wedding
Mrs. Lillian Hudson and Joseph R. Wheeler were quietly married Tuesday afternoon, Jan. 11.

regions.label: text
regions.text:
NORTH SIDE. NEWS
By Mrs. Earl Gordon

The Butler Community Center of 1088 North West Street meets the first Thursday of the month, in the citizenize of the North Side, invited by Dr. A. G. Fairfax, president; Mrs. G. D. F Ashborn, executive director; Mrs. Ada Hunt and Mrs. Mamye Harris of 7876 Ernz court entertained Sunday, evening, Jan. 16, at a dinner party. Mrs. George Stewart of Evanston. Mrs. Mrs. Schussese and Miss Sissie. All North West Street was mailed or phoned to North Side office, 4652 Winthrop avenue, phone Edgewater 8858. To wish to correct the error in last week's issue that Master Robert W. Bostick did not play for the Community Club, played at Herman Baptist Church Christmas. David Brown gave a supper party at the North West Street. His guests were Miss Anna Mae Cooper, Miss Alice Brown, Miss Hazel and Lora Hawkins. Benn Hanson of 1246 North Wells took unto himself a better half last Wednesday. Benn has moved his business from Wells and Division streets. John Clark, owner of a big garage on North West Street, and Lawrence avenue, planning holiday in the area. Verce Craton of 1150 Cambridge avenue surprised her husband by giving a party on his birthday last Tuesday night. Mrs. Freemon of 1343 North Wells street is ill. Randolph Mitchell of 1346 North Wells street is expecting to move his place of business over to the North West Street. Benn Bunch of 3332 Craft street gave a dinner party last Saturday night. Rev. Mr. Jones, D. D., of Evanson spoke three nights last week at the Tabermacle Church, 523 West Division Street. There was a large crowd present at the North West Club. 523 West Street is no longer under the management of James Simpson, Jones and Harrison, as W. H. Harrison has assumed the full ownership, and guarantees every one the best of service.

regions.label: paragraph_title
regions.text: The University Society

regions.label: text
regions.text: The University Society met at 3032 South Wahash avenue Sunday afternoon, Jan. 9, in the first semi-annual meeting of the new year. It was also the beginning of their tenth year of existence. Miss Sophia Boaz, the retiring president, appointed Harris B. Gaines to the office of officers. Dr. Williama Williams acted as attorney Gaines, who was also to be placed in office. The officers to be control the destinies of the society for the next six months are as follows: E. O. Marshall, president; Miss Lenora Curtis, vice president; Miss Randolph, secretor; C. E. Parish, treasurer; H. Owens, journalist; Attorney Harris E. Gaines, parliamentarian, and Irvin A. Jackson, reporter.

regions.label: paragraph_title
regions.text: Phyllis Wheatley

regions.label: text
regions.text: Miss Bertha Palm left for her home, Bristol, Oklah, on account of illness in the family. * Miss Katie Kelley was home last week on account of illness. She attended a donation to the soup kitchen at Borsel Church. * The girls of the club sent Mrs. E. L. Davis a potted plant. They went in a body and sang for her. * Prof. J. Mundy's opera class enjoyed a charming solo rendered by Miss Nelle Dobson, who has a charming soprano voice. On Monday, the fourth Sunday in every month. A very interesting program has been arranged. All are invited.

regions.label: paragraph_title
regions.text: Health Editor Speaks In Ohio

regions.label: text
regions.text:
Dr. A. Wilberforce Williams, specialist and health editor of the Chicago Defender, will speak in the Y. M. C. A. building of Cincinnati Sunday on Jan. 10 under the auspices of the office Home on the School Association. The association is making a "drive" for the purpose of raising money to purchase a large hospital and training school and has invited Dr. Williams as speaker of the day. Dr. Williams will return Monday, Jan. 24, on the job looking after his very extensive and lucrative practice.

NEVER COVET BEAUTY! USE YVONNE.-Advt.

regions.label: text
regions.text:
BY J. Wesley Jones
Wiley O'Kelly visited his sister, Mrs. Eustace Elliott. He is now living in Canada and having great success. He was formerly a member of Providence Baptist Church Chork and the leading bass singer. *Miss Cleo Mae Dickerson returned home this week after spending the holidays in Kansas City and St. Louis. Ms. She reports a pleasant trip. *Roy B. J. Prince, the pastor of the Original Presbyterian Church, is having a very successful revival meeting. He will be assisted by his brother of Texas. *The Friendship Baptist Church, of which Rev. Drane is pastor, continues to have large crowds. *Park Avenue C. M. E. Church has a big musical the first Sunday in February. It will be given by the Prophet John O'Reilly. As the church society has such large following, the church is issuing free tickets, which any one can get by calling or writing for them. Mrs. Parker is manager. *Mrs. I. H. Holloway, the head of our West Side physician, Dr. L. H. Holloway, continues to improve. She has been about 24 months. *Mrs. W. H. Harvey, about 12 months. *Mrs. W. H. Harvey, and prominent member of Fulton Street M. E. Church is home slick. *J. Wesley Jones received a letter last week from Prof. H. P. B. Johnson, the music director of National Jazz Convention. It will be remembered by Prof. Johnson made a big bit in Columbus. *Henry Chorus. He with the other Nashville people are planning to entertain the National Association of Negro Musicians, which convenes in that city in July. *St. Stephen Chork, under director of Prof. Turner, gave an interesting musical presentation. It is reported that Mr. Turner expects to have these musicals once a month. *Hon. James A. Scott, orator, scholar, author, statesman and recent assistant state's attorney, will address the Sunday Musical Sunday, Jan. 16th at 8:30 p. m., at the Original Provident Church. His subject, "The Constitution and Its Meaning to the Negro." The music, under the direction of Prof. Albert H. Jones, will have its usual artistic touch. Dr. L. H. Holloway will preside, as usual.

regions.label: paragraph_title
regions.text: Will Hold Memorial Services

regions.label: text
regions.text: Memorial services for Miss Mary G. Chadwick, for many years a teacher in South Division and Wendell Phillips High School, Monday, Jan. 24, at 7:45 p. m. Former students and alumni are especially invited.

regions.label: text
regions.text:
Editor, Addresses: Musical
In an address before the monthly
Sunday night Prof. Willis; N. Huggins,
teacher in Chicago public schools,
editor of the Upreach Magazine, div-
orted from his set speech on history
and literature and drove home neeeded
counsel on business. "Out of 150 boy",
said Editor Huggins, "who gave
choices of the work they would like to
do when grown up, 95 per cent of them
gave 'blind, alley' types of careers,
in which there were little chances for
success, we were actually down."
Prof. Huggins urged larger patronage
of our business enterprises, as means
of giving employment to trained young
men and women.

regions.label: paragraph_title
regions.text: William Russell Dies

regions.label: text
regions.text: William Henry- Russell, brother of the well known dramatic critic and editor of the Chicago-Star, Sylvester Russell, died suddenly of pneumonia in New York. City Jan. 10. He leaves his children, Jessica and Sade Boyens of New York, and Mrs Anna Marie Holt of Milwaukee, Wis. to mourn his loss.

regions.label: paragraph_title
regions.text: Smith, Boys, Hold, Reunion

regions.label: text
regions.text:
Mr. Smith, Hays.
Mr. and Mrs. Guilford Smith of Casper, Wyo., are the guests of Mr. Smith's mother, Mrs. Bessie Smith. 3249 South Park avenue. Last Friday there was a reunion of the song, La-Rue: Otis Smith coming from Springfield, IL, to join the family. The third son, Robert, Ivies with his mother.
Another Lawyer Makes Appearance.
Mr. and Mrs. Herman Barnett. 3624 Grand boulevard, are the proud parents of Robert, Ivies born at Provident Hospital, when he and his mother are getting along fine.

regions.label: paragraph_title
regions.text: Misa Hogan Entertainment

regions.label: text
regions.text: Miss Lenora Hogan, 652 East 29th street, gave a dinner party in honor of Miss Florence-Bernest of Champaign. III. These present were Mr. and Mrs. B. B. Hogan, Mrs. Josephson and Iva McDowell and Messrs. Willie Miller and James Sweeney.

regions.label: text
regions.text:

regions.label: text
regions.text: Epworth League Symposium. Last Sunday at South Park M. E Church, under the auspices of the Epworth League, the symposium by editors and publishers, on "What are the advantages of puzzles, in the way of the advancement of people and what should they do to make was a success. The place was crowded and people standing. Children, and young people made up two-thirds of the audience and gave appreciative attention. The able discussions by Nathan Daniel Brascher, editor in chief of the Negro Associated Press, who last edited; A. Rogers, author and editor of Enterprise; Wills Huggs, editor and editor for the Up-Ench Magnazine; William Moore post, author and editor of the Pulman view and on the editorial staff of the Negro Associated Press, and Mrs. Ira R. Stuart of Toledo, Ohio, contained helpful material for a constructive program for our young folk. Community singing, led by Dr. M. B. White, nucleus Alexia, director of the community, and Mrs. Cooper, with Mrs. Ophile Wells, pianist, added much to the enjoyment of the program. Miss Esther Foulkes and Mrs. Arnett assisted Epworthians with the small people and ushers.

regions.label: text
regions.text:
The next symposium is Jan. 30 with the same subject. Women heading our largest organizations for the social, moral- and civic improvement of our people will discuss the subject.
